problems.problem:
A recipe calls for flour and sugar in a ratio of $5:3$ by weight. If a baker uses $12$ ounces of sugar, how many ounces of flour are needed?

A) $16$
B) $18$
C) $20$
D) $24$

A car travels $210$ miles on $7$ gallons of gasoline. At this rate, how many gallons are needed to travel $375$ miles?

A) $10.5$
B) $12.5$
C) $13.5$
D) $15.0$

step_by_step.step: Find the rate of fuel consumption

problems.problem:
On a map, $1.5$ inches represents $24$ miles. Two cities are $7$ inches apart on the map. What is the actual distance, in miles, between the cities?

A) $98$
B) $105$
C) $112$
D) $126$

Three workers can paint a house in $10$ days, all working at the same constant rate. How many workers, working at that same rate, are needed to paint the house in $6$ days?

A) $4$
B) $5$
C) $6$
D) $8$

step_by_step.step: Compute the total work in worker-days
Divide by the new number of days

problems.problem:
The ratio of boys to girls in a school is $7:8$. There are $1{,}200$ students in total. How many girls are there?

A) $560$
B) $600$
C) $640$
D) $680$

step_by_step.step: Find the number of parts in the ratio

step_by_step.explanation: Girls make up $\frac{8}{15}$ of the total. $\frac{8}{15} \times 1200 = 640$.

problems.problem:
A jacket originally priced at $\$85$ is on sale for $30\%$ off. An $8\%$ sales tax is then applied to the sale price. What is the final cost, in dollars?

A) $\$59.50$
B) $\$62.45$
C) $\$64.26$
D) $\$66.30$

A town's population increased from $24{,}000$ to $27{,}600$ over five years. What was the percent increase?

A) $10\%$
B) $12\%$
C) $15\%$
D) $18\%$

step_by_step.step: Divide by the original and convert to percent

problems.problem:
A stock decreased in value by $20\%$ one year, then increased by $25\%$ the following year. What is the net percent change from the original value?

A) $5\%$ decrease
B) $5\%$ increase
C) $0\%$
D) $10\%$ increase

step_by_step.explanation: Start at $100$. After $20\%$ decrease: $100 \times 0.80 = 80$. After $25\%$ increase: $80 \times 1.25 = 100$.

problems.problem:
In a survey, $42\%$ of respondents said they prefer online shopping. If $546$ respondents preferred online shopping, how many respondents were surveyed in total?

A) $1{,}100$
B) $1{,}200$
C) $1{,}300$
D) $1{,}400$

A store increases the price of a television by $15\%$ and then offers a $15\%$ discount during a sale. The final price is what percent of the original price?

A) $85\%$
B) $97.75\%$
C) $100\%$
D) $102.25\%$

A cyclist rides at a constant speed of $18$ miles per hour. What is the cyclist's speed in feet per second? ($1$ mile = $5{,}280$ feet)

A) $22.0$
B) $24.2$
C) $26.4$
D) $28.8$

step_by_step.step: Convert miles per hour to feet per hour, then per second

problems.problem:
A tank holds $45$ liters of water. Approximately how many gallons does the tank hold? ($1$ gallon $= 3.785$ liters)

A) $10.6$
B) $11.9$
C) $13.2$
D) $14.5$

step_by_step.step: Divide liters by the conversion factor

problems.problem:
A runner completes a $10$-kilometer race in $42$ minutes $30$ seconds. What is the runner's average pace in minutes per mile, to the nearest tenth? ($1$ mile $\approx 1.609$ km)

A) $6.2$
B) $6.5$
C) $6.8$
D) $7.1$

step_by_step.step: Convert distance to miles and time to minutes

step_by_step.explanation: $\frac{42.5}{6.215} \approx 6.84$ minutes per mile, which rounds to $6.8$.

problems.problem:
A rectangular field measures $80$ meters by $120$ meters. What is its area in square kilometers?

A) $0.0096$
B) $0.096$
C) $0.96$
D) $9.6$

Fabric costs $\$12$ per square yard. What is the cost, in dollars, of a piece of fabric that is $6$ feet wide and $15$ feet long? ($1$ yard = $3$ feet)

A) $\$90$
B) $\$108$
C) $\$120$
D) $\$140$

step_by_step.step: Compute area in square feet, then convert to square yards
Multiply by cost per square yard

problems.problem:
A museum tracked its daily visitors over four days:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Day} & \text{Visitors} \\ \hline \text{Thursday} & 340 \\ \hline \text{Friday} & 510 \\ \hline \text{Saturday} & 680 \\ \hline \text{Sunday} & 470 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What was the mean number of visitors per day?

A) $470$
B) $500$
C) $510$
D) $520$

step_by_step.step: Divide by the number of days

problems.problem:
A line graph shows the noon temperature over five consecutive days: Monday $58$°F, Tuesday $62$°F, Wednesday $55$°F, Thursday $68$°F, Friday $72$°F. What was the increase in temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit, from Wednesday to Friday?

A) $14$
B) $17$
C) $20$
D) $23$

A frequency table shows the number of books read by participants in a summer reading program:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Books Read} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 0 & 3 \\ \hline 1 & 7 \\ \hline 2 & 8 \\ \hline 3 & 5 \\ \hline 4 & 2 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the median number of books read?

A) $1$
B) $2$
C) $3$
D) $2.5$

step_by_step.step: Find the total number of participants
Locate the median position in the cumulative frequencies
step_by_step.explanation: Cumulative: $0$→$3$, $1$→$10$, $2$→$18$. The $13$th participant is in the $2$-book group.

problems.problem:
A scatterplot shows the relationship between hours studied and exam score for $15$ students. The line of best fit has equation $S = 8h + 52$, where $S$ is the predicted score and $h$ is hours studied. What is the predicted score for a student who studies $4.5$ hours?

A) $80$
B) $84$
C) $88$
D) $92$

A circle graph shows a school's annual budget of $\$800{,}000$. Instruction receives $45\%$, facilities $20\%$, and administration $15\%$. The remainder is allocated to other expenses. How many dollars are allocated to other expenses?

A) $\$120{,}000$
B) $\$140{,}000$
C) $\$160{,}000$
D) $\$180{,}000$

step_by_step.step: Find the percent for other expenses

problems.problem:
The ages of participants in a study are: $22, 25, 29, 31, 34, 34, 38, 42, 45, 55$. What is the median age?

A) $32.5$
B) $34$
C) $35.5$
D) $36$

step_by_step.explanation: $n = 10$ (even). The median is the average of the $5$th and $6$th ordered values.

problems.problem:
A dataset of $7$ values has a mean of $15$. Six of the values are $11, 13, 14, 16, 18, 19$. What is the seventh value?

A) $12$
B) $13$
C) $14$
D) $15$

step_by_step.step: Calculate the total sum from the mean
Subtract the sum of the known values

problems.problem:
The monthly rents for $10$ apartments are analyzed. The mean is $\$1{,}450$ and the median is $\$1{,}350$. If the rent of the most expensive apartment increases by $\$500$, how are the mean and median affected?

A) Both the mean and median increase.
B) The mean increases; the median stays the same.
C) The mean stays the same; the median increases.
D) Both the mean and median stay the same.

step_by_step.step: Analyze the effect on the mean
step_by_step.explanation: The total sum increases by $500$, so the mean increases by $\frac{500}{10} = 50$.

step_by_step.step: Analyze the effect on the median
step_by_step.explanation: The most expensive apartment is at the high end of the ordered list. Increasing its value does not change which value occupies the middle position, so the median is unchanged.

problems.final_answer: B) The mean increases; the median stays the same.

problems.problem:
A set of five numbers has a range of $18$ and a mean of $12$. The smallest number is $3$. What is the largest number?

A) $15$
B) $18$
C) $21$
D) $24$

Dataset A has a standard deviation of $4.2$. Dataset B is formed by adding $10$ to each value in Dataset A. What is the standard deviation of Dataset B?

A) $4.2$
B) $10.0$
C) $14.2$
D) Cannot be determined from the information given.

step_by_step.step: Understand the effect of adding a constant
step_by_step.explanation: Adding a constant shifts all values equally. The spread of the data — measured by standard deviation — does not change.

problems.problem:
Box plots summarize test scores for two classes. Class A has median $78$ and IQR $12$. Class B has median $82$ and IQR $20$. Which class has greater variability in the middle $50\%$ of scores?

A) Class A, because its median is lower.
B) Class A, because its IQR is smaller.
C) Class B, because its IQR is larger.
D) Class B, because its median is higher.

step_by_step.explanation: The interquartile range (IQR) measures the spread of the middle $50\%$ of the data.
Class B has an IQR of $20$, compared to Class A's $12$. Larger IQR means greater variability.

problems.final_answer: C) Class B, because its IQR is larger.

problems.problem:
A dataset displaying household incomes in a city is skewed to the right. Which relationship between the mean and median is most likely?

A) Mean $<$ Median
B) Mean $=$ Median
C) Mean $>$ Median
D) Mean $\le$ Median

step_by_step.step: Recall the effect of skew on measures of center
step_by_step.explanation: In a right-skewed distribution, the tail on the right pulls the mean upward. The median is less affected by extreme values and stays closer to the peak.

problems.problem:
A histogram of student heights is approximately symmetric and bell-shaped, with a mean of $65$ inches and a standard deviation of $3$ inches. Approximately what percent of students have heights between $59$ and $71$ inches?

A) $68\%$
B) $90\%$
C) $95\%$
D) $99.7\%$

step_by_step.step: Express the boundaries in terms of standard deviations
step_by_step.explanation: $59 = 65 - 2(3)$ and $71 = 65 + 2(3)$. The interval covers $\pm 2$ standard deviations.
In a normal distribution, approximately $95\%$ of data falls within $2$ standard deviations of the mean.

problems.problem:
The five-number summary of a dataset is: minimum $= 10$, $Q_1 = 20$, median $= 30$, $Q_3 = 45$, maximum $= 80$. What is the interquartile range?

A) $15$
B) $25$
C) $35$
D) $70$

A histogram of the ages of $200$ survey respondents shows a peak at ages $30$–$39$ and a long tail extending to the right. Which measure of center is likely the largest?

A) The mode
B) The median
C) The mean
D) All three measures are approximately equal.

step_by_step.step: Identify the shape of the distribution
step_by_step.explanation: A peak with a long right tail is a right-skewed distribution.

step_by_step.step: Recall the ordering of measures in a right-skewed distribution
step_by_step.explanation: Mean $>$ median $>$ mode. The mean is pulled farthest into the tail.

problems.problem:
A bag contains $5$ red, $3$ blue, and $4$ green marbles. One marble is selected at random. What is the probability it is NOT red?

A) $\frac{5}{12}$
B) $\frac{7}{12}$
C) $\frac{1}{2}$
D) $\frac{5}{7}$

step_by_step.step: Find the total number of marbles
Count the non-red marbles and find the probability

problems.problem:
A standard six-sided die is rolled twice. What is the probability that the sum of the two rolls is $8$?

A) $\frac{1}{9}$
B) $\frac{5}{36}$
C) $\frac{1}{6}$
D) $\frac{7}{36}$

step_by_step.step: Divide by the total number of outcomes

problems.problem:
In a class of $30$ students, $18$ study Spanish and $12$ study French. Five students study both languages. What is the probability that a randomly selected student studies exactly one of these languages?

A) $\frac{7}{15}$
B) $\frac{1}{2}$
C) $\frac{2}{3}$
D) $\frac{5}{6}$

step_by_step.step: Find the number studying exactly one language
step_by_step.explanation: Spanish only: $18 - 5 = 13$. French only: $12 - 5 = 7$. Exactly one: $13 + 7 = 20$.

problems.problem:
A jar contains $4$ quarters and $6$ dimes. Two coins are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that both coins are quarters?

A) $\frac{1}{5}$
B) $\frac{4}{25}$
C) $\frac{2}{15}$
D) $\frac{1}{3}$

A survey finds that $60\%$ of residents own a car and $35\%$ own a bicycle. If $20\%$ own both, what is the probability that a randomly selected resident owns a car given that they own a bicycle?

A) $\frac{1}{3}$
B) $\frac{4}{7}$
C) $\frac{3}{5}$
D) $\frac{2}{5}$

A study surveys $500$ members of a fitness center and finds that $82\%$ exercise at least three times per week. The researchers conclude that most adults in the city exercise frequently. Which best evaluates this conclusion?

A) The conclusion is valid because the sample size is large enough to represent the city.
B) The conclusion is valid because the percentage who exercise frequently is overwhelming.
C) The conclusion is flawed because the sample is biased toward a group already likely to exercise.
D) The conclusion is flawed because the survey did not ask what types of exercise respondents do.

step_by_step.explanation: The survey was conducted at a fitness center — respondents are already people who exercise. This is a biased, non-representative sample.
A fitness center sample cannot be generalized to all city adults. Choice C correctly identifies the selection bias.

problems.final_answer: C) The conclusion is flawed because the sample is biased toward a group already likely to exercise.

problems.problem:
A study claims a new energy drink improves concentration. Thirty employees drank the drink before a cognitive test, and their average score was higher than the national average. There was no control group, and participants knew they were testing the energy drink. What is the most significant flaw?

A) The sample size of $30$ is too small to produce any meaningful result.
B) The study lacks a control group and blinding, so the placebo effect cannot be ruled out.
C) The cognitive test may not have accurately measured concentration.
D) Employees may not be representative of all people who consume energy drinks.

step_by_step.explanation: Without a control group receiving a placebo and without blinding, any observed effect could be due to participants' expectations rather than the drink itself.

problems.final_answer: B) The study lacks a control group and blinding, so the placebo effect cannot be ruled out.

problems.problem:
A researcher wants to estimate the average height of high school seniors in a state with $450$ high schools. Which sampling method would produce the most reliable estimate?

A) Measuring the heights of all seniors at the researcher's local high school.
B) Asking each high school to send the heights of its five tallest seniors.
C) Randomly selecting seniors from a complete list of all seniors in the state.
D) Surveying the first $200$ seniors who volunteer to participate in the study.

step_by_step.step: Identify the method that minimizes bias
step_by_step.explanation: Simple random sampling from the full population gives every senior an equal chance of selection. Choices A, B, and D all introduce selection bias.

problems.final_answer: C) Randomly selecting seniors from a complete list of all seniors in the state.

problems.problem:
A headline reads, "Study Shows Eating Breakfast Doubles Test Scores!" The study actually found that students who ate breakfast averaged $82\%$ on tests, compared to $78\%$ for those who skipped breakfast. Which best evaluates the headline?

A) The headline is accurate because any increase in test scores is educationally meaningful.
B) The headline misrepresents the finding by exaggerating a difference of $4$ percentage points as a doubling.
C) The headline is accurate because the study used a randomized controlled design.
D) The headline correctly interprets the practical significance of the study's results.

step_by_step.step: Compare the headline to the actual data
step_by_step.explanation: The difference is $82\% - 78\% = 4$ percentage points — a small relative difference, not a doubling.
The headline exaggerates the size of the effect in a misleading way.

problems.final_answer: B) The headline misrepresents the finding by exaggerating a difference of $4$ percentage points as a doubling.

problems.problem:
A study reports a strong correlation ($r = 0.72$) between monthly ice cream sales and the number of drowning deaths in a city. A news report concludes that eating ice cream causes drowning. Which is the best evaluation of this conclusion?

A) The conclusion is justified because the correlation is strong.
B) The conclusion is justified because sales data and mortality data are both reliable.
C) The conclusion confuses correlation with causation; warm weather is a likely confounding variable that increases both ice cream sales and swimming-related drownings.
D) The conclusion would be valid if the study had also measured ice cream consumption at the individual level.

step_by_step.explanation: Correlation does not imply causation. A third variable — warm summer weather — likely drives both ice cream sales and swimming activity (and thus drowning risk).

problems.final_answer: C) The conclusion confuses correlation with causation; warm weather is a likely confounding variable that increases both ice cream sales and swimming-related drownings.

problems.problem:
The two-way table shows student enrollment in art and music classes at a high school.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Art} & \text{No Art} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Music} & 35 & 25 & 60 \\ \hline \text{No Music} & 20 & 40 & 60 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 55 & 65 & 120 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

What is the probability that a randomly selected student takes Art given that they take Music?

A) $\frac{35}{120}$
B) $\frac{35}{60}$
C) $\frac{35}{55}$
D) $\frac{60}{120}$

step_by_step.explanation: $P(\text{Art}|\text{Music})$ means we look only at the Music row ($60$ students).

problems.problem:
Using the same table, what percent of students who take Art also take Music? (Round to the nearest percent.)

A) $58\%$
B) $64\%$
C) $69\%$
D) $73\%$

The table shows survey results on voting by age group.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Voted} & \text{Did Not Vote} & \text{Total} \\ \hline 18\text{–}29 & 48 & 72 & 120 \\ \hline 30\text{–}49 & 90 & 60 & 150 \\ \hline 50+ & 140 & 40 & 180 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 278 & 172 & 450 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which age group had the highest proportion of voters?

A) $18$–$29$
B) $30$–$49$
C) $50+$
D) All three groups had the same proportion.

step_by_step.step: Calculate the proportion for each group

step_by_step.explanation: The $50+$ group has the highest proportion.

problems.problem:
Using the voting survey table, what is the probability that a randomly selected person is aged $30$–$49$ given that they voted?

A) $\frac{90}{150}$
B) $\frac{90}{278}$
C) $\frac{150}{450}$
D) $\frac{90}{450}$

step_by_step.explanation: Given that the person voted, we look only at the Voted column (total $278$).

problems.problem:
The table shows pet ownership among $100$ surveyed residents.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Dog} & \text{No Dog} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Cat} & 18 & 12 & 30 \\ \hline \text{No Cat} & 24 & 46 & 70 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 42 & 58 & 100 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Based on the data, are owning a dog and owning a cat independent?

A) Yes, because $P(\text{Dog}) = P(\text{Dog}|\text{Cat})$.
B) Yes, because more people own neither pet than own both.
C) No, because $P(\text{Dog}) \neq P(\text{Dog}|\text{Cat})$.
D) No, because more than half of residents do not own a dog.

step_by_step.explanation: Since $0.60 \neq 0.42$, the events are not independent.
